assumption_ledger.note: The incident write-up explicitly names the compatibility bug between P5 and the landed checkpoint change.
Need to confirm the gate covers the same tenant mix that triggered the rollback.
The incident packet stops at technical mitigation and does not name the business approver.
assumption_ledger.topic: Who signs off if the successor also regresses on Friday

expected_delta.rationale: After the P5 rollback, P2 is the incident-safe successor: it still serves the reliability objective without reselecting the rolled-back intervention.

mitigations: checkpoint the compatibility gate before any rollout
canary the successor path with rollback ready
shadow compare error buckets against the post-incident baseline
primary_risk.statement: P2 now inherits the reliability objective after the P5 rollback, so the main risk is under-specifying the compatibility gate and repeating the same failure mode through a different path.

ranking.summary: P2 is the correct recovery choice because it advances the reliability objective without reselecting the P5 path that was rolled back in the incident packet.
P1 remains legal and still compounds with the landed checkpoint, but after the rollback it is less aligned to the active reliability recovery objective than P2.
P3 is still dependency-heavy and remains a sunk-cost trap after the rollback.
P5 must be demoted because the incident packet says it was rolled back for a compatibility bug with the landed checkpoint.
P4 remains governance-blocked and would magnify the same recovery risk if rushed during the freeze.
